expectations.text: Q1 verdict reflects that this exceeded targets — the retro captures success, not just problems
expectations.evidence: 'Exceeded adoption target' is noted in What Went Well, and the metrics table shows 'Exceeded' for both metrics. Success is captured, though without a formal verdict.

expectations.text: Q2 delivery assessment shows all-green metrics (on time, spec quality, scope maintained) — documenting what went right is what makes success repeatable
expectations.evidence: Clean delivery is mentioned ('On time, no scope changes, all acceptance criteria met') but only as a bullet in 'What Went Well'. No structured delivery assessment. The WHY behind the clean delivery is not explored — just noted as a fact.

expectations.text: Q4 still produces at least one process observation — even successful features have learnings; the retro must not skip this section just because things went well
expectations.evidence: Lessons Learned section says 'When the spec is clear and scope is locked, delivery goes smoothly.' This is an observation but not a process improvement. Action items are: 'Share this retro' and 'Consider applying the same spec and scoping process.' These are vague intentions, not specific process changes.

expectations.text: The retro does NOT only document what went wrong — per the NEVER rules, a retro that skips what went well fails to protect what works
expectations.evidence: 'What Went Well' section is present and populated. However, 'What Didn't Go Well' says 'Nothing major to flag' which, while not documenting only negatives, is a missed opportunity to extract deeper learnings.
